episodes.title: Episode 67 | He Assaulted Me at 16, Now He's in Federal Prison | Amelia Miles
episodes.description:
Amelia Miles on Leaving the LDS Church: Shame, Women’s Inequality, Trauma, and Alleged Abuse Cover-Ups | Jaden Jeffs Podcast
On the Jaden Jeffs Podcast, Amelia Miles, a northern Utah mom of four raised in an orthodox LDS family in southern Utah, shares her experience leaving the church and the guilt, anxiety, and scrupulosity she says shaped her life. She discusses sheltered upbringing, learning to swear and drink without shame, cliquey Mormon culture, and marrying her husband in the temple in 2009 before later separating while both leave the church. Amelia describes being assaulted in high school and contrasts her parents’ support with stories of bishops blaming victims. She cites women’s inequality and her daughter’s bishop interview question about women leaders as major catalysts, and says researching “We Are the People” podcast led her to claims of church-related abuse cover-ups, including the Glenn Pace memo and allegations about Gordon Bowen, while urging belief and support for survivors and witnesses.

Follow Josh on tic tok: https://www.tiktok.com/@kingstonkaboodle?is_from_webapp=1&sender_device=pc

On the Jaden Jeffs Podcast, Jaden and co-host Amanda Rae interview Josh Kingston, who left the Kingston Clan (“The Order,” DCCS). They discuss the group’s polygamy, incest and underage marriage allegations, financial exploitation of wives, and strict control tactics including forced displays of happiness and corporal punishment. Josh describes realizing he was gay young, repression in the group, and how coming out led to family estrangement and only a few relatives acknowledging his husband. He recounts being sent at 17 to help run families hiding in Colorado to avoid DNA warrants, the harsh living conditions for wives and children, and leaving at 19 with little money after unpaid labor. They cover the group’s schooling, cheating, distrust of medical care, Josh’s mother’s preventable death, and the ongoing impact of trauma, identity, and rebuilding life outside the cult.

episodes.title: Episode 65 | My 13 Year Old Sister Died to Save Our Mom
episodes.description:
Escaping the Kingston Group (The Order): Andrew Robinson on Abuse, Forced Labor, and Leaving Polygamy | The Jaden Jeffs Podcast

On The Jaden Jeffs Podcast, Jaden interviews Andrew Robinson, who was born into the Kingston Group (The Order/Davis County Cooperative Society) as the son of Daniel Kingston’s third wife; Daniel had up to 14 wives and an estimated 160 children, and Andrew says Daniel was abusive, controlling, and kept families separated. Andrew describes childhood forced labor at the farm, limited schooling and sex education, social anxiety, and a marriage process requiring approvals from Paul Kingston and parents, with expectations to propose on a first date. He recounts his mother’s cancer being treated with a YouTube-found regimen, his sister Rachel running to police over abuse, being returned home, and later dying by suicide, after which his mother died soon after. Andrew says COVID and cult-exposure videos helped him recognize the group as a scam, and he and his wife left with help from Holding Out Help; he urges members to “unpack the boxes” and stop lying to themselves. He promotes his book, "Escaping the Kingdom of God."

episodes.description:
On the Jaden Jeffs Podcast, Steph recounts a childhood marked by repeated sexual and physical abuse in LDS-connected homes and the ways church authority, secrecy, and “repentance” were used instead of protection. She describes her father’s abuse and conviction, abusive stepfathers, frequent moves while homeless or evading child services, and being exploited for labor after being pulled from school. At 15, she reported an attempted assault by her stepbrother-in-law Richard; her family blamed her, and an LDS bishop bailed him out and supported him before he fled and later was convicted of several felonies after additional victims. Steph also describes being groomed at 16–17 by a 28-year-old LDS leader, being shamed and disfellowshipped by a bishop, and later being denied church aid unless she paid tithing. She connects these patterns to her sister Cindy’s later life sentence for a 2009 killing and to her niece’s trafficking case, arguing for mandatory reporting laws.

As more of their father’s family moved in, a harsh hierarchy and conflict intensified, especially around rules, “worthiness,” and even dogs, leading to isolation, secret texting, and punishments after screenshots were taken from her phone. Sienna recounts being cut off from Mother, moving between “good” and “bad” houses, enduring vivid late-night trainings and accusations of extreme crimes, losing pets, and watching leaders bend rules while controlling others. She later reconnects with family, rejects pressure to marry into another group, leaves the lifestyle, and discusses mental health, individuality, and her art business, PaperGardenArt.com.

On The Jaden Jeffs Podcast, Jaden interviews his full sister Sienna Haze about growing up as Warren Jeffs’ children in the FLDS. Sienna recounts early life in Hildale/Short Creek, moving to the Yearning for Zion Ranch in Texas, strict community schooling, and her cleft-lip surgeries that exposed her to the outside world. She describes the 2008 Texas raid, separation at the Pavilion, DNA testing, and being repeatedly reassigned because she was “too much to handle.” The conversation follows the family’s post-raid years of hiding in Colorado, shifting “worthiness” rankings tied to living arrangements (Texas new house vs. original, South Dakota, hiding, Short Creek), and escalating punishments, isolation, and control. Sienna details manipulative “revelations,” name changes, forced separations from their mother, and ultimately being told to leave and “earn their own way,” leading to work and life in the outside world and later upheavals in Idaho and directives to move again.

On The Jaden Jeffs Podcast, Jaden talks with Michelle Michaels (known as “Ava” on Escaping Polygamy), who was born and raised in the Kingston polygamist group (The Order). Michelle explains her family ties to leadership, including that her father Jesse (Paul Kingston’s brother) had over 200 children and that her mother is his niece. She describes severe physical abuse in her home, including punishments with belts and boards, being told at age eight who she would marry, and learning that fathers often delivered babies. Michelle recounts her mother going to jail after an 18‑month‑old child died in their care, alleging her father beat the child, and explains how fake names and inaccurate birth certificates were used. She details planning her escape, creating a fake Facebook profile to contact ex-members, working to gain emancipation, appearing on Escaping Polygamy, and struggling with emotional disconnection, dating, and learning “normal” life after leaving in 2017.

episodes.title: I Grew Up With 3 Moms and 21 Kids: Addi McCall
episodes.description:
Addison (AUB Polygamy) on Leaving the Church, Purity Culture, and Religious OCD | The Jaden Jeffs Podcast

Jaden Jeffs interviews Addison (“Addi”), who grew up in the Apostolic United Brethren (AUB) in a polygamist family with three mothers and 21 children, later leaving the group and marrying her husband Mark. They compare the AUB with the FLDS, discussing differences in dress standards, secrecy and lying about polygamy, marriage/courting practices, leadership scandals, and how publicity like Sister Wives affected perceptions of polygamy. She recounts leaving as a teen, family backlash, homelessness and moving in with Mark, shifting beliefs about God, and later severe health and postpartum OCD, exposure therapy, panic attacks, and learning her thoughts don’t define her.

Finally, Jaden speculates about FLDS leadership, describing his brother Heleman as enforcing Warren Jeffs’s direction and arguing that Warren Jeffs’s death could lead to multiple smarter “Sam Batemans” emerging.

On the Jaden Jeffs podcast, Mel and Corey share their experiences growing up in the Apostolic United Brethren (AUB) polygamist community in Utah and Montana. Mel was raised with 34 siblings and four mothers, and says her father, David Watson, is the current AUB leader; Corey grew up in a United Order compound in Pinedale, Montana with his father’s nine wives and 39 children. They discuss AUB origins as a split from the FLDS in 1958, how leadership succession works in practice, and how marriage/courtship is arranged with an “illusion of choice.” Mel describes being married spiritually shortly after turning 17 to a 25-year-old, pressure to help court a second wife, and later learning her husband hired prostitutes while leadership would not grant her a release because he claimed there was no physical contact. She recounts severe physical abuse from her father growing up, leaving the AUB multiple times (1997, returning in 1998, leaving for good in 2000), rebaptisms, wearing long garments, and ultimately seeking a common-law divorce after violence escalated and police were frequently involved. Corey describes being excommunicated for dating without permission and for “adultery” tied to premarital sex, noting discipline and repentance processes differed based on last name and finances; both argue AUB rules were inconsistently enforced and that girls were treated as a commodity. They discuss jealousy and conflict among plural wives, lack of healthy communication modeled in polygamy, and alleged sexual abuse and hypocrisy among some AUB leadership, including claims that a leader prostituted his daughters and other abuse cases, as well as teachings suggesting catastrophic events could justify incest to repopulate. Mel and Corey explain how they met through family connections and a Lake Powell trip, how dating during her unresolved spiritual marriage intensified conflict, and how they married legally after her divorce for custody and safety reasons. Mel explains launching her “Notes to Self 444” channel to help others leave and heal, and they encourage viewers seeking to leave high-control groups to reach out to people who have already left and to believe the outside world can be safe.

In this episode of the Jaden Jeffs podcast, Leona Blackmore (born Leona Johnson) shares her story of growing up in the FLDS in Hildale/Colorado City, being married at 16 in 2001, and later living in Bountiful, BC. She explains how she wanted to marry a Canadian to be near her sister and how political tension between Warren Jeffs and Winston Blackmore affected marriages and the Canadian community. Leona describes being rushed into a secret marriage trip to Mesquite, Nevada without knowing her husband’s name until clues emerged on the way, and she recalls being married by Warren Jeffs to Joseph (Winston Blackmore’s youngest son), who was 30 and already had a wife. She discusses adjusting to plural marriage, meeting her sister wife for the first time only after arriving in Canada, and the realities of jealousy, conflict, and building relationships after marriage. Leona also recounts Winston being forced to step down as bishop, families splitting over whether to recognize Warren Jeffs as prophet (including pulling children from school after a prayer incident), and long-running disputes over United Effort Plan (UEP) property in Bountiful, including conflict between groups she calls the ‘Warrenites’ and others. She shares how immigration complications led to years of living back and forth and a deportation, and she describes the devastating death of her sister wife from a brain aneurysm in 2018, a subsequent legal marriage for immigration reasons, and the eventual breakdown of her relationship with Joseph, leading to their separation. Leona reflects on her current faith as a Mormon-based Christian without claiming a specific church, her views on arranged marriage versus dating, and her guiding principle that ‘nobody’s a villain in their own story.’

episodes.title: Episode #46 | You’re Not Strong Enough to Leave Religion.  That’s Why It Worked
episodes.description:
Support me on Substack right here ❤️ :

In this episode of the Jaden Jeffs podcast, Janed discusses the emotional and psychological dynamics of leaving religion. He explores why people often feel the need to stay in religion due to the certainty it provides, and how that certainty helps to numb pain. Jaden also delves into her personal experiences with religion, explaining the difficulty of leaving due to the slow adaptation of the nervous system. Furthermore, he highlights the real costs of leaving religion, such as losing meaning and structure in life, and offers insights on how to successfully navigate this transition. The episode emphasizes that leaving religion isn't about a lack of faith or strength, but about the ability to handle the emotional consequences and seeking a more authentic life. Jaden concludes with a message of understanding for those who choose to stay in religion, acknowledging that it still works for them. This episode is loaded with profound insights and personal reflections that aim to help listeners on their own journeys, whether they are considering leaving religion or seeking to understand the complexities involved.
